rows.item: 学習意欲・授業参加度

summary: The course will use both a pragmatics theory approach and instructional pragmatics methodology. Students will learn foundational theory which they will later apply to student-led projects related to teaching and learning pragmatics in the EFL classroom. Students enrolled in this course will increase their pragmatic knowledge, specifically, learning how to use English that is socially appropriate to match the situation. By improving pragmatic knowledge and awareness, overall communicative competence will increase.
aims: This course aims to provide students with an overview of the field of pragmatics within the context of learning and teaching English as a foreign language (EFL).
goals: Students enrolled in this course will gain an understanding of pragmatics within the field of foreign language instruction.

Students enrolled in this course will develop the ability to analyze pragmatic features of language use in real communicative contexts, including speech acts, politeness, and intercultural interaction.
Students enrolled in this course will examine important research and key ideas in instructional pragmatics and consider how they can be used in foreign language teaching.

plan.text: During the introductory meeting, students will be introduced to the field of pragmatics and how what we say, how we say it, and the context in which we speak can influence the meaning and interpretation of language. During this initial meeting, we will also learn some of the relevant technical vocabulary that will be used throughout the course. Students will also learn the expectations of the course and a detailed explanation of the class readings and student-led projects.

This class meeting will be an introduction to speech acts. Since many students will grasp the concept that a speech act can be, for example, an invitation or a request, it is important that students gain an understanding of speech act theory and further understand the importance of examining speech acts in both their pure communicative form and possible intended and perceived forms.

This class will expand on the Speech Act Theory with an explanation of locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary acts. Students will become aware of the how speech acts have literal meaning, intended meaning and perceived meaning based on the context and how the utterance is conveyed. Some speech act samples will be shared, and students will write possible meanings. Finally, some authentic examples from films will be used to reinforce the concept.

Building on the previous two classes, this lesson will introduce some common situational elements that require different approaches. Some common situational elements include relative age, social position, gender, occupation, and level of imposition. Students will complete tasks in small groups related to examining these situational elements in their L1. Small groups will then create some examples in their L1 accompanied by an explanation in English. Finally, the class will examine some examples in English and discuss the various considerations for each situation. This class is intended to provide students with some practical experience in creating scenarios for the term project.

The beginning of the class will start with a review and instructor-led discussion of the material covered in the previous weeks. This tutorial will help prepare for the group task work and preview how the tutorial class activity in Week Nine will proceed. In the remainder of the class, students will work in small groups to examine authentic examples of speech acts in films and explain the possible meaning of these acts and recognize how situational elements impacted the utterances.

Speech act sets are possible strategies that can be used when navigating different speech acts. This class will examine different speech act sets, including apologizing, requesting, agreeing, and disagreeing. For each speech act set, students will examine various scenarios and decide the best strategy for each scenario. The scenarios used in this class will be provided by the instructor and an explanation of the potential that more than one answer can be appropriate will be discussed.

Using polite language is an important element of successful communication, especially when learning and teaching foreign languages. Politeness theory (Brown and Levinson, 1978) is based on the idea that politeness strategies used when communicating depend on who you are speaking with, your social relationship to that person, and on the topic.

Building on the previous week’s class and within the topic of politeness in communication, this class will introduce the concept of face. This class will introduce strategies for positive politeness and negative politeness.
The tutorial class will mark the end of Unit 1 of the course. The tutorial class is a review of the course material covered in the first eight classes.
Student Project 1 - The first student-led project will span two class periods and students will have the opportunity to demonstrate their grasp of the concepts covered in the class
This class meeting will serve as a review of the theory and concepts learned in Unit 1 and how theory can be applied to teaching in a practical manner.
Student Project 2 - The second student project will consist of students creating a lesson to teach speech acts to a specific group of learners.. The final reflection report will be assigned.
Student Project 2 - Continued - In the final class meeting students will give demonstration lessons.

textbooks:
Textbook
Kasper, G. and Rose, K.R. (2001) Pragmatics in Language Teaching. Cambridge University Press.
Reference readings*
Bardovi-Harlig, K. and Mahan-Taylor, R. (2003). Introduction to Teaching Pragmatics. English Teaching Forum, Vol. 41, No 3. pp. 37-39
Siegal, et al. (2000) Saying it ‘just right’: Teaching for pragmatic success in ELT. ELT Journal 73 (1), pp. 31-40
*Other readings will be assigned during the semester.
prereq: There no Prerequisites for this seminar
prep: It is important that students actively participate in the class, ask questions, and take notes. Outside of class, students should review their notes and keep with all assigned readings so they can fully participate in class discussions
